questions.question: What does EEG primarily measure?

options.B: Summed post-synaptic dendritic field potentials of pyramidal cells
options.C: Blood oxygenation changes in the brain
options.D: Magnetic fields generated by neural activity

questions.explanation: This is a classic trap question. EEG measures the summed post-synaptic dendritic field potentials of pyramidal cells — NOT action potentials. Pyramidal cells are aligned in parallel, allowing their electrical fields to summate and be detected at the scalp.

questions.question: What is the key distinction between perturbation methods and neuromonitoring methods?

options.A: Perturbation methods are non-invasive; neuromonitoring is invasive
options.B: Perturbation methods test causality; neuromonitoring provides correlational evidence
options.C: Perturbation methods have better temporal resolution
options.D: Neuromonitoring methods can only be used in animals

questions.explanation: Perturbation methods (lesions, TMS, tDCS, optogenetics, pharmacology) disrupt brain function to test whether a region is causally necessary. Neuromonitoring methods (EEG, fMRI, PET, MEG) observe brain activity and provide correlational evidence.

questions.question: What is diaschisis?

options.A: A brain imaging technique using radioactive tracers
options.B: The disruption of function in brain areas remote from but connected to a lesion site
options.C: A type of brain stimulation that enhances cognitive function
options.D: The overlap between two different lesion locations

questions.explanation: Diaschisis refers to the disruption of function in brain regions that are distant from but connected to the site of a lesion. This makes interpreting lesion studies difficult because the observed deficit might not be solely due to damage at the lesion site.

questions.question: What is the approximate temporal profile of the BOLD response in fMRI?

options.A: Onset at 5-6 sec, peak at 10 sec, return at 20 sec
options.B: Onset at 1-2 sec, peak at 5-6 sec, return at 12-15 sec
options.C: Instantaneous onset, peak at 1 sec, return at 3 sec
options.D: Onset at 10 sec, peak at 15 sec, return at 30 sec

questions.explanation: The hemodynamic response function (HRF) underlying the BOLD signal has an onset at 1-2 seconds after neural activity, peaks at approximately 5-6 seconds, and returns to baseline at about 12-15 seconds.

questions.question: Which method is described as creating 'virtual lesions'?
questions.explanation: TMS (Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation) is described as creating 'virtual lesions' because it can temporarily disrupt neural activity in a targeted cortical region, mimicking the effects of a brain lesion without permanent damage.

questions.question: What does the 'inverse problem' refer to in neuroimaging?

options.A: The difficulty of determining brain sources from scalp-recorded signals
options.B: The delay between neural activity and the BOLD response
options.C: The trade-off between spatial and temporal resolution
options.D: The problem of reverse inference in brain imaging studies

questions.explanation: The inverse problem refers to the mathematical difficulty of determining the exact brain sources that generated the signals recorded at the scalp. Multiple different source configurations can produce the same pattern of scalp recordings. This applies to both EEG and MEG.

questions.question: What distinguishes anodal from cathodal tDCS?

options.A: Anodal uses magnetic fields; cathodal uses electric fields
options.B: Anodal increases cortical excitability; cathodal decreases it
options.C: Anodal is used on the left hemisphere; cathodal on the right
options.D: Anodal is more precise than cathodal

questions.explanation: In tDCS (transcranial direct current stimulation), anodal stimulation increases cortical excitability in the targeted region, while cathodal stimulation decreases it. This allows researchers to either enhance or suppress neural activity.

questions.question: Why has PET been largely supplanted by fMRI?

options.A: PET has better spatial resolution but worse temporal resolution
options.B: PET requires radioactive isotopes, uses task blocks, and has poor temporal resolution
options.C: PET can only image subcortical structures
options.D: PET is more expensive than fMRI

questions.explanation: PET requires injecting radioactive isotopes, relies on block designs (comparing task blocks), and has essentially no temporal resolution for tracking moment-to-moment neural dynamics. fMRI offers better spatial and temporal resolution without radiation.

questions.question: What does MVPA stand for and what does it do?

options.A: Multi-Voxel Pattern Analysis — classifies patterns across many voxels
options.B: Multiple Visual Processing Assessment — tests visual cortex function
options.C: Magnetic Variance Pulse Acquisition — improves fMRI signal quality
options.D: Motor-Visual Pathway Analysis — traces motor-visual connections

questions.explanation: MVPA (Multi-Voxel Pattern Analysis) uses pattern classification algorithms to decode information from distributed patterns of activity across many voxels, going beyond traditional analyses that look at activity in individual brain regions.

questions.question: What is the central trade-off in neuroimaging methodology?
questions.explanation: The spatial vs temporal resolution trade-off is THE central concept in neuroimaging methodology. Methods like EEG have excellent temporal resolution (milliseconds) but poor spatial resolution, while fMRI has good spatial resolution (millimetres) but poor temporal resolution (seconds).
